source_paper_bibtex: @article{Ahmadi_2025, title={Robust-to-Dynamics Optimization}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0116}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0116}, number={2}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Ahmadi, Amir Ali and Günlük, Oktay}, year={2025}, month=may, pages={965–992} }
context:
Section 4 (Future directions and a broader agenda), page 34

For example, is the problem of testing membership of a point to the feasible set of an R-LD-LP decidable? We have shown that this problem is NP-hard in general and polynomial-time solvable when the spectral radius of the linear map is bounded away from one. It follows from [5] that the general problem is decidable when n \le 3. In higher dimensions, however, decidability is unknown even in the special case where the input polyhedron is a single halfspace.
title: Decidability of feasible-set membership for robust-to-linear-dynamics linear programs
background_latex:
Consider discrete-time linear dynamics on \(\mathbb{R}^n\) given by \(x_{k+1}=Gx_k\), where \(G\in\mathbb{Q}^{n\times n}\) is a rational matrix. Let the constraint set be a (rational) polyhedron \(P=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^n: Ax\le b\}\), with \(A\in\mathbb{Q}^{m\times n\), \(b\in\mathbb{Q}^m\), and inequalities interpreted componentwise.

Define the set of \(P\)-invariant initial conditions under \(G\) by
\[
S := \bigcap_{k=0}^{\infty}\{x\in\mathbb{R}^n : G^k x \in P\} 
   = \bigcap_{k=0}^{\infty}\{x\in\mathbb{R}^n : A G^k x \le b\}.
\]
Equivalently, \(x\in S\) if and only if the entire forward orbit \(\{G^k x\}_{k\ge 0}\) stays in \(P\).

The associated decision problem (membership problem) takes as input \((A,b,G,z)\) with \(z\in\mathbb{Q}^n\) and asks whether \(z\in S\). This is the feasibility question underlying robust-to-linear-dynamics linear programs (R-LD-LPs), i.e., linear programs with the additional constraint that iterates under \(G\) remain in \(P\) forever.
problem_definition_latex:
Given rational data \(A\in\mathbb{Q}^{m\times n}\), \(b\in\mathbb{Q}^m\), \(G\in\mathbb{Q}^{n\times n}\), and \(z\in\mathbb{Q}^n\), decide whether
\[
z \in S := \bigcap_{k=0}^{\infty}\{x\in\mathbb{R}^n : A G^k x \le b\}.
\]
Equivalently, decide whether \(AG^k z\le b\) holds for all integers \(k\ge 0\).

location_in_paper: Section 4 (Future directions and a broader agenda), page 34
open_problem_quote: For example, is the problem of testing membership of a point to the feasible set of an R-LD-LP decidable? We have shown that this problem is NP-hard in general and polynomial-time solvable when the spectral radius of the linear map is bounded away from one. It follows from [5] that the general problem is decidable when n \le 3. In higher dimensions, however, decidability is unknown even in the special case where the input polyhedron is a single halfspace.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The problem is a decidability question over all rational instances, not a claim that can be refuted by a single finite counterexample within a bounded search space. While one can search for hard instances empirically, negative results would require reductions, and positive results would require a terminating decision procedure, neither of which is well supported by brute-force instance search.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: An evolutionary coding loop could explore candidate decision procedures or special-case algorithms and test them on large randomly generated instances by simulating orbits up to increasing horizons, but such testing cannot certify decidability or correctness on all inputs. It could still be useful to discover heuristics, invariants, or reductions to known decidable subcases that may guide theory.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The membership problem for robust-to-linear-dynamics linear programs asks, given rational \(A,b,G,z\), whether the entire forward orbit \(G^k z\) remains in the rational polyhedron \(P=\{x:Ax\le b\}\), i.e., whether \(AG^k z\le b\) for all \(k\ge 0\). Ahmadi and G\"unl\"uk show this problem is NP-hard and highlight that decidability is known in low dimension (via results on the semialgebraic orbit problem) but is open in general, even for a single halfspace constraint. This places the question at the interface of invariant-set computation, linear recurrence decision problems, and logic/number theory.

The strongest partial positive result comes from work on the semialgebraic orbit problem, which yields decidability when \(n\le 3\). Beyond low dimension, the landscape is shaped by classic decision problems for linear dynamical systems and linear recurrences: Skolem-type questions (zero-testing) and positivity/ultimate-positivity problems for linear recurrence sequences are closely related to checking inequalities of the form \(a^T G^k z\le b\). Recent work on positivity refines what is decidable in these recurrence settings and may provide routes to decidable subclasses (e.g., restrictions on eigenstructure, diagonalizability, or sign patterns).

At the same time, undecidability results for matrix semigroups (multiple matrices) underscore a sharp boundary: allowing time-varying/uncertain linear dynamics can make membership undecidable, suggesting that any general decidability proof for the single-matrix case must exploit commutativity and the rigid algebraic structure of powers \(G^k\). Promising directions include reducing halfspace invariance to decidable fragments of the theory of real algebraic numbers under spectral restrictions, extending low-dimensional semialgebraic-orbit techniques to higher dimensions, and clarifying the precise relationship to Skolem-Pisot/positivity problems for the scalar sequences \(a_i^T G^k z-b_i\).

sources.title: The semialgebraic orbit problem
sources.theorem_or_result: Proves decidability of the semialgebraic orbit problem for linear dynamical systems, yielding (as noted in the source paper) decidability of R-LD-LP feasible-set membership when the state dimension satisfies \(n\le 3\).
sources.connection: Membership \(z\in S\) is an invariance question for the orbit \(\{G^k z\}\) relative to a polyhedron \(P\). The semialgebraic orbit framework decides whether an orbit hits/avoids semialgebraic sets; in low dimension this implies decidability of whether all iterates remain in \(P\).

sources.title: What’s Decidable About Discrete Linear Dynamical Systems?
sources.theorem_or_result: Survey/chapter organizing decidability frontiers for discrete linear dynamical systems, including orbit, reachability, and positivity problems closely connected to invariance and constraint satisfaction along \(G^k z\).
sources.connection: Places the membership question \(AG^k z\le b\ \forall k\) among classic decision problems for linear recurrences and linear dynamical systems, clarifying which algebraic/number-theoretic obstacles (e.g., Skolem-type problems) block general decidability results.

sources.title: The power of positivity
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops decidability/complexity results for positivity properties of linear recurrence sequences and related linear dynamical systems questions.
sources.connection: Inequalities \(a_i^T G^k z\le b_i\) can be rewritten as sign/positivity constraints on linear recurrence sequences derived from \(G\). Positivity results inform special cases where membership might be decidable or reducible to known decidable fragments.

sources.title: The presence of a zero in an integer linear recurrent sequence is NP-hard to decide
sources.theorem_or_result: Shows NP-hardness of deciding whether a linear recurrence sequence hits zero, a Skolem-type decision problem.
sources.connection: Ahmadi--Günlük reduce R-LD-LP membership hardness from a related NP-hard problem; Skolem-type problems are also cited as structurally connected to halfspace invariance and membership in \(S\).

sources.title: Improved matrix pair undecidability results
sources.theorem_or_result: Strengthens undecidability results for problems about products of two matrices (matrix semigroups).
sources.connection: While the open problem concerns a single matrix \(G\), undecidability for multiple matrices underlies the contrast drawn in the source paper: membership becomes undecidable for uncertain/time-varying dynamics, suggesting that proving decidability for the single-matrix case may require delicate number theory.

sources.title: A survey of computational complexity results in systems and control
sources.theorem_or_result: Survey of complexity/decidability results for control and dynamical systems problems, including spectral radius and joint spectral radius questions.
sources.connection: Provides context for why algorithmic questions about linear dynamics with rational data can be hard or undecidable, and situates the membership problem among known complexity barriers.
